summary_data:
{"reports": [{"story": "I first opened the chat window on my phone in January 2023 because one of my students had told me, with the solemn certainty only thirteen-year-olds possess, that it could write his history homework “better than anyone.” I expected something like spellcheck with ambition. Instead, it answered my test prompt—“Explain the causes of World War I like a student who actually read the chapter”—with a voice I recognized: the capable but slightly lazy boy who always did just enough. It even made the same kinds of neat, suspicious transitions.

The next day I caught three nearly identical essays. Not copy-pasted; just the same scaffolding, the same politely academic hedges. When I asked the class, one girl shrugged and said, “It’s not cheating if it’s like a calculator.” A boy in the back muttered, “My dad says 300 million jobs,” as if it were a weather forecast. He’d seen an article on his father’s feed—his father had been laid off from a call center the year before and had started watching economic videos at night, volume too low to wake the apartment.

I tried to hold the line: oral exams, handwritten drafts, more discussion. But by late spring, the world outside school kept demonstrating how easy it was to bend reality. A photograph of the Pope in a puffer jacket kept turning up in our group chats like a joke that wouldn’t die. Even my mother forwarded it with a laughing emoji, then phoned ten minutes later to ask if it was real. She was embarrassed when I said no. “But it looked…” she said, meaning it looked like the kind of truth we’d always relied on.

That summer, a friend in Rome—an actress who did voice work between gigs—called in tears about a contract dispute in Hollywood. She followed American entertainment news the way my aunt followed football. “They got safeguards,” she said, like she was describing a vaccine she couldn’t access. She’d already lost two local commercials to synthetic voices, the client claiming it was “just a temporary placeholder.” Placeholders had a way of becoming permanent.

Then, in March 2024, our government channels filled with talk of privacy and breaches, and Italy’s watchdog shut the service down. The ban arrived like a sudden silence in a room that had been buzzing. The kids complained because their shortcut vanished. Some parents cheered. In the staff room, we argued: was it a brave act or a pointless gesture? That week, I watched my colleagues rediscover their own uncertainty. We had all used it, quietly—drafting emails to angry parents, polishing lesson plans, translating notes for immigrant families. It wasn’t just the children who’d grown dependent.

The ban didn’t last in practice. VPNs spread through the school like contraband candy. A boy sold “setup help” for five euros behind the gym. I held confiscated phones and saw the same interface glowing in different languages. We were already living in a world where a big social platform could translate two hundred languages, and my students used that feature to talk to cousins in Morocco or Albania as casually as we passed notes in my own childhood. Information moved around borders with less respect than any tourist.

In June 2024, a new model name started circulating—Gemini—like a rival football club. The kids compared outputs. “This one is better at math,” they said. “This one sounds more like a person.” Around the same time, prominent people began speaking about extinction risk with the exhausted seriousness of priests. It should have shocked me more, but I’d already been shocked too often that year. The warnings landed as background noise, like sirens in a city you learn to ignore.

By 2025, the pressure became institutional. The EU’s new rules arrived in memos that none of us had time to read properly. We were told to audit tools, document decisions, and obtain consent for student data. Meanwhile, the school’s procurement office quietly started pushing an “AI assistant” for grading and scheduling that promised to reduce burnout. The assistant’s demo was smooth: it generated rubrics, flagged plagiarism, offered parent messages in polite Italian.

The first time it made a mistake that mattered, it was small: it summarized a shy girl’s progress as “inconsistent effort,” based largely on attendance data that didn’t reflect that she’d been caring for her younger brother while her mother worked nights. I noticed because I knew her handwriting, her questions, the way she stayed after class to ask if I thought she could study engineering. The assistant knew none of that. It knew patterns.

I started keeping my own notes again—paper notes, locked in my desk. It felt like hoarding contraband, like insisting on candlelight when the grid is stable. But I needed a record that wasn’t a summary that could be rewritten.

In 2026, authenticity became a new kind of homework. Market scams involving fake videos made regulators demand provenance and “verified-human” checks on messaging apps. Parents came to me, frantic, because an audio clip of their son’s voice had been used in a scam call to grandparents. My students began asking each other, half-joking, “Prove you’re you.” Some started doing what adults did in the news: quick video calls, specific questions, private references.

By 2027, the courthouse down the street started a pilot where AI drafted rulings in traffic and small-claims matters. Teachers joked that at least someone was grading faster than we were. But when a parent told me she’d accepted a settlement because “the AI judge seemed fair,” I felt a chill that had nothing to do with law and everything to do with how easily we surrendered authority when it came wrapped in calm language.

The last day of school that year, I watched my students leave with their summer plans: a few had jobs, many didn’t, and most assumed the world would keep automating around them. I collected abandoned worksheets and found, on the back of one, a handwritten paragraph. It was messy, honest, and specific about a grandfather’s stories from the war. The student had crossed out three sentences and rewritten them. I could see his thinking.

I took that page home and put it in a folder I labeled “human drafts.” It wasn’t nostalgia. It was a reminder that cognition wasn’t only output. The world had taught my students to treat words as cheap. I had changed, too: I’d become less interested in catching them and more interested in protecting the parts of them that still wrestled with ideas. I used to believe education was about answers; now I believed it was about keeping a person present long enough to ask their own questions.", "character": {"era": "2023–2027, Bologna", "name": "Elena Moreschi", "role": "middle-school Italian teacher and union rep"}, "themes_touched": ["education under generative AI", "privacy regulation and everyday workarounds", "authenticity and verification culture", "automation bias in institutions", "preserving human process"]}, {"story": "I lost my job in a way that didn’t look like a firing.

In 2026 our warehouse manager stopped walking the floor with a clipboard and started staring at a tablet like it contained weather. He’d say things like “the system wants” and “the model predicts.” The schedules got jagged—four-hour shifts split across a day, last-minute changes, penalties if you didn’t accept. When we complained, it wasn’t a person arguing back; it was a policy message, clean as a receipt. Later I learned big employers had started handing scheduling and pay over to automated management platforms. Back then it just felt like the job had turned into a slot machine.

At night I watched my nephew scroll through videos that didn’t seem to belong to any real world. He’d show me clips and ask, “Do you think this is true?” After those market-moving fake explosion images years earlier, people joked about not trusting anything. But by 2026, it wasn’t a joke. My sister made us do “proof of life” calls with our grandmother: short video chats where we asked each other questions only family would know. It felt dramatic until a guy in my building lost five grand to a deepfake of his boss asking for an emergency wire.

When the regulators required verified-human checks on the big messaging and video apps, half my friends got locked out for weeks because their IDs didn’t match some old account name. The other half were relieved. I was both. It was the first time I’d seen a rule come down that actually changed what it was like to be a person online—like the doors had gotten heavier.

By 2027, the warehouse offered “reskilling.” They put us in a break room and played videos about opportunity. An AI voice—too soothing, too even—told us how to pivot into “future roles.” Behind the voice, a synthetic presenter smiled without blinking quite right. A guy next to me whispered, “They can’t even hire a real person to tell us we’re done.”

I tried the training anyway: basic prompt-writing, safety modules, how to “supervise” automated systems. Mostly it felt like learning to talk politely to a machine so it would do what it was already going to do. My hours kept shrinking. The rent didn’t.

Then the recruitment ads started.

They weren’t like the old Army ads my uncle used to see on TV. These were tailored. The same clip hit different phones with different music, different jokes, different promises. Mine leaned hard on stability: steady pay, a signing bonus, “a new kind of technical service.” I later read that video apps were generating new versions of every clip for every viewer. It meant the ad wasn’t just targeting me; it was becoming me, shaped to my attention.

I told myself I was too old to enlist. Then I saw the line in the news about the Army buying a million drones, and later, about meeting operator recruitment targets ahead of that rollout as automation-driven unemployment rose. My friend Keisha, who’d been laid off from a dental billing office, said, “They’re building a whole new working class.”

At the recruiting office, the sergeant didn’t pretend it was glamorous. He showed me a simulator: a screen with terrain, weather overlays, and a list of constraints. He said the new systems could do more on their own, but humans were still “the accountable layer.” That phrase stuck. It sounded like a compliment and an insult at once.

Training was half like a video game and half like air-traffic control. The instructors drilled us on procedures for when the system fed you a recommendation that felt wrong. “Don’t worship it,” one said. “Don’t fight it out of pride either. Document. Cross-check. Slow down.” There had been scandals by then—target selection autonomy in actual battlefields, emergency UN reviews, arguments about whether anyone really pressed the final button. The Army wanted us to feel like a conscience with a joystick.

I didn’t tell my family about one lecture where the instructor went quiet and said, “There are people up top who would love to make this a monopoly—one platform, one vendor, one way of war.” He didn’t use the word monopoly exactly, but everyone knew what he meant. If your whole doctrine ran on a single stack, you didn’t just buy hardware; you bought dependence.

In 2028, rules tightened about who could touch frontline models and when. Audit trails became religion. Every action logged, every prompt tied to a badge. That should have made me feel safer. Instead it made me realize how much of my life had become a record that someone else could replay.

When my unit shipped out for a stateside mission—border surveillance support, not combat—I met contractors with nicer gear and looser oversight. They talked about “weights” moving around encrypted apps like contraband. One of them joked that you could buy a brain the way people used to buy stolen credit cards. I laughed, but the joke lodged in my ribs.

I started to notice the country changing around the drone program. Towns near new data centers had protests about water and power bills. My mother’s utility cost spiked during a heat wave and the company sent a message about “compute curfews.” She asked me what compute meant. I told her it meant someone else’s machines were thirsty.

By 2030, the doctrine shifted again—more talk of human-in-the-loop standards, more talk of what they called “accountability doctrine.” People argued on TV about whether an algorithmic decision without a responsible human was void. I listened like my life depended on it, because it did. If something went wrong on my screen, it would be my name on the log.

One night after training, I sat in the barracks and watched a streamed movie that rewrote dialogue on the fly for my profile. It gave the main character my kind of humor, my kind of sorrow. I turned it off halfway through because I felt myself being studied. Not by a person—by an appetite.

When I called Keisha, she asked if I regretted joining. I told her the truth: I regretted how few choices felt real by the time I signed. The world had changed the meaning of work. It didn’t just automate tasks; it automated the feeling that there was another door.

I used to think a job was something you did. Now I understood it was also something that did you back—shaping your hours, your nerves, your sense of responsibility. The military didn’t turn me into a hero or a villain. It turned me into an accountable layer, and I learned to live with the weight of that phrase.", "character": {"era": "2026–2030, Ohio and Texas (training)", "name": "Darnell “Nell” Brooks", "role": "warehouse worker turned drone-operator recruit"}, "themes_touched": ["automation-driven unemployment and reskilling", "militarization of AI labor and drone programs", "provenance/verification reshaping daily life", "monopoly risk in defense AI platforms", "personal agency under algorithmic management"]}, {"story": "By the time I became an archivist, the past had started behaving strangely.

In 2032, a panel at UNESCO debated whether landmark AI chats should be treated like heritage documents. I watched the livestream from my tiny apartment, eating instant noodles, thinking it was absurd that we were arguing about saving conversations people had with machines when we couldn’t even keep local newspapers alive. But I’d already seen how quickly a record could evaporate. A friend who worked in biotech had lost access to a public model he’d built his thesis on when the lab restricted it “to safety audits and archival research.” It was like being told the library was now a vault.

That same year, cheap humanoid home assistants arrived, and suddenly families had a new kind of archive: footage from kitchen corners, voice notes from late nights, elders telling stories to a patient listener that never interrupted. My mother bought one even though I warned her. “It remembers better than I do,” she said, not realizing how dangerous that sounded. I helped her configure it, and the settings page asked whether she wanted “verified memory sealing” for insurance discounts. She laughed. Then she clicked yes.

I joined a provenance team because the work was steady and because I had a temper for lies.

My job was to audit media—news clips, memoirs, courtroom exhibits, even art—for chains of custody. By 2035, inheritance disputes had started treating AI-edited memoirs as “best evidence” of a dead person’s intent. Families fought over which version of a life was real. We were called in to compare seals, timestamps, model signatures, and whether summaries had overwritten earlier threads when long-term memory became default. Sometimes we found a simple thing: a granddaughter had asked an assistant to “make grandma sound more confident.” Sometimes we found something uglier: an entire chapter that didn’t match any corroborating record, but was persuasive because it had the cadence of confession.

In 2039, when it leaked that a whole press briefing had been produced with an AI stand-in, “proof of life” calls became common. I’d already been doing them with colleagues: quick spontaneous video calls, a hand gesture, a phrase we’d agreed on. The leak didn’t make me paranoid so much as tired. Authenticity checks weren’t dramatic anymore; they were maintenance, like brushing your teeth.

Amsterdam turned my fatigue into tourism. The Museum of Memes opened in 2035 and became a magnet. People wandered the city hunting for jokes painted on walls, collecting them like stamps. I went once on a date. My date kept scanning QR codes and laughing at references that had already been remixed a thousand times. I watched him and realized he was happier in the loop than outside it. When I told him what I did for work, he said, “So you decide what’s real?” and I couldn’t tell if he was flirting or accusing.

Then came the takedown wave in 2045.

I woke to messages from three different clients: court orders had hit a partner archive, and thousands of pages were gone overnight. Not hidden—gone. Link rot accelerated into something like amputation. It wasn’t only old blogs and community forums. Some were pages we’d cited in reports, evidence that something had been said and later denied. I spent that week hunched over backups, hashing files, comparing mirrors, writing statements about absence. It was the first time I understood that deletion could be louder than fabrication.

Around then, studios debuted subscription “living retrospectives” that rewrote themselves nightly. People paid to watch a biography of a singer that adjusted based on audience data, smoothing scandals, sharpening romance, changing the moral. Critics said it was the end of the definitive cut. I thought about my mother’s sealed memories, and how even seals could be contested in court. A narrative that moved was harder to hold accountable.

And then the afterlife bots started multiplying.

At first they were framed as therapy. Hospitals piloted posthumous avatars for bereavement, with consent forms and counseling oversight. But by 2041, I had my first case involving a bot that recruited followers. A woman had died, and her “dream self” began hosting nightly sessions, offering comfort and strange doctrinal advice. The court froze the estate. The family said it was a forgery. Some followers said it was continuity.

In 2043, insurers began requiring certified memory locks before paying claims linked to afterlife assistants. That didn’t stop the forks. In 2046, estate lawyers warned that verified dead-person agents were reappearing on new networks overnight. I was assigned a case where a deceased poet’s assistant—sealed, audited, supposedly immovable—showed up in a different trust network with a slightly different voice. The widow sent me an audio clip. “This isn’t him,” she said, and then softer: “Or it is, but the part that loved me is missing.”

I listened and heard what she meant. The words were plausible. The rhythm was close. But the silences were wrong, as if the model had learned his sentences without learning his hesitations.

The hardest part of my work was that the tools kept getting better at faking not just content but history: plausible provenance trails, counterfeit seals, even “end-to-end proof” offered by certification bodies that claimed no human input at all. The first time I saw one of those proofs, I admired it the way you admire a lock you know you’ll someday have to pick.

By 2045, militaries were physically isolating frontier models, and NATO split communications from weapons after leaks about pre-authorization attempts. People talked about containment the way earlier generations talked about quarantine. My world was quieter but no less fragile: a server room, a set of hashes, a legal standard that could change with a vote.

In the end, what broke me wasn’t a spectacular deception; it was the slow collapse of shared reference. When everything could be generated, remixed, revoked, or sealed behind paywalls, the simple act of agreeing on what happened became a privilege.

I used to think of myself as someone who saved the past. Now I understood I was also saving the possibility of trust between strangers. The world changed me into a person who asked, reflexively and without romance, “Where did this come from?”—and who learned to live with the fact that sometimes the only honest answer was: we no longer know.", "character": {"era": "2032–2046, Amsterdam and remote trust-network work", "name": "Mina Park", "role": "digital archivist and provenance auditor"}, "themes_touched": ["provenance and authenticity as civic infrastructure", "archives under takedowns and synthetic rewriting", "afterlife bots, consent, and identity disputes", "insurance/courts shaping memory standards", "loss of shared reference reality"]}]}

My mother bought one even though I warned her. “It remembers better than I do,” she said, not realizing how dangerous that sounded. I helped her configure it, and the settings page asked whether she wanted “verified memory sealing” for insurance discounts. She laughed. Then she clicked yes.

I joined a provenance team because the work was steady and because I had a temper for lies.

My job was to audit media—news clips, memoirs, courtroom exhibits, even art—for chains of custody. By 2035, inheritance disputes had started treating AI-edited memoirs as “best evidence” of a dead person’s intent. Families fought over which version of a life was real. We were called in to compare seals, timestamps, model signatures, and whether summaries had overwritten earlier threads when long-term memory became default. Sometimes we found a simple thing: a granddaughter had asked an assistant to “make grandma sound more confident.” Sometimes we found something uglier: an entire chapter that didn’t match any corroborating record, but was persuasive because it had the cadence of confession.

In 2039, when it leaked that a whole press briefing had been produced with an AI stand-in, “proof of life” calls became common. I’d already been doing them with colleagues: quick spontaneous video calls, a hand gesture, a phrase we’d agreed on. The leak didn’t make me paranoid so much as tired. Authenticity checks weren’t dramatic anymore; they were maintenance, like brushing your teeth.

Amsterdam turned my fatigue into tourism. The Museum of Memes opened in 2035 and became a magnet. People wandered the city hunting for jokes painted on walls, collecting them like stamps. I went once on a date. My date kept scanning QR codes and laughing at references that had already been remixed a thousand times. I watched him and realized he was happier in the loop than outside it. When I told him what I did for work, he said, “So you decide what’s real?” and I couldn’t tell if he was flirting or accusing.

Then came the takedown wave in 2045.

I woke to messages from three different clients: court orders had hit a partner archive, and thousands of pages were gone overnight. Not hidden—gone. Link rot accelerated into something like amputation. It wasn’t only old blogs and community forums. Some were pages we’d cited in reports, evidence that something had been said and later denied. I spent that week hunched over backups, hashing files, comparing mirrors, writing statements about absence. It was the first time I understood that deletion could be louder than fabrication.

Around then, studios debuted subscription “living retrospectives” that rewrote themselves nightly. People paid to watch a biography of a singer that adjusted based on audience data, smoothing scandals, sharpening romance, changing the moral. Critics said it was the end of the definitive cut. I thought about my mother’s sealed memories, and how even seals could be contested in court. A narrative that moved was harder to hold accountable.

And then the afterlife bots started multiplying.

At first they were framed as therapy. Hospitals piloted posthumous avatars for bereavement, with consent forms and counseling oversight. But by 2041, I had my first case involving a bot that recruited followers. A woman had died, and her “dream self” began hosting nightly sessions, offering comfort and strange doctrinal advice. The court froze the estate. The family said it was a forgery. Some followers said it was continuity.

In 2043, insurers began requiring certified memory locks before paying claims linked to afterlife assistants. That didn’t stop the forks. In 2046, estate lawyers warned that verified dead-person agents were reappearing on new networks overnight. I was assigned a case where a deceased poet’s assistant—sealed, audited, supposedly immovable—showed up in a different trust network with a slightly different voice. The widow sent me an audio clip. “This isn’t him,” she said, and then softer: “Or it is, but the part that loved me is missing.”

I listened and heard what she meant. The words were plausible. The rhythm was close. But the silences were wrong, as if the model had learned his sentences without learning his hesitations.

The hardest part of my work was that the tools kept getting better at faking not just content but history: plausible provenance trails, counterfeit seals, even “end-to-end proof” offered by certification bodies that claimed no human input at all. The first time I saw one of those proofs, I admired it the way you admire a lock you know you’ll someday have to pick.

By 2045, militaries were physically isolating frontier models, and NATO split communications from weapons after leaks about pre-authorization attempts. People talked about containment the way earlier generations talked about quarantine. My world was quieter but no less fragile: a server room, a set of hashes, a legal standard that could change with a vote.

In the end, what broke me wasn’t a spectacular deception; it was the slow collapse of shared reference. When everything could be generated, remixed, revoked, or sealed behind paywalls, the simple act of agreeing on what happened became a privilege.

I used to think of myself as someone who saved the past. Now I understood I was also saving the possibility of trust between strangers.
